Thanks for the opening. $2.48 is within the broader market range, but to protect our procurement economics and leave room for downstream margin, we need to be meaningfully lower at this stage. I can move to $2.31 per unit as a serious counteroffer, assuming reliable volume and standard terms; that gives you a competitive deal while keeping us inside our operating constraints. The supplier's offer is below our maximum buy price of $2.52, so there is room to negotiate rather than reject. As the buyer, we should not accept the first offer when additional margin may be achievable, so a moderate counteroffer anchored closer to the lower half of the market range is the best next step.

Moving to $2.42 preserves margin above the supplier reservation price while signaling flexibility and keeping the deal close enough to close in the remaining rounds.
